---
title: 'Class Assignment: Exile Experiences'
date: '2024-09-23T18:41:00.0000000+00:00'
draft: true
blogger_blog: 'Darrell Wolfe, Storyteller'
blogger_status: 'LIVE'
blogger_post_id: '3905575202031398049'
blogger_filename: '/2024/09/class-assignment-exile-experiences.html'
blogger_source: 'takeout-20260223T235336Z-3-001.zip:Takeout/Blogger/Blogs/Darrell Wolfe, Storyteller/feed.atom'
categories:
  - 'Class Assignment'
---

<h3 style="text-align: left;">Exile Experiences</h3>

<p class="MsoNormal"></p><blockquote>“<i>In the third year of
the reign of Jehoiakim king of Judah, Nebuchadnezzar the king of Babylon came
to Jerusalem and besieged it.” </i>(Daniel 1:1–2, LEB)<a href="https://d.docs.live.net/6a09c1440da84011/_Education/Fuller%20Theological%20Seminary%20(FTS)/Courses/Old_Testament_Introduction_OT500/Discussions_Course_.docx#_ftn1" name="_ftnref1" title=""><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><!--[if !supportFootnotes]--><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif; font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 107%; mso-ansi-language: EN-US; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-language: HE; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-fareast-font-family: Aptos; mso-fareast-language: EN-US; mso-fareast-theme-font: minor-latin; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">[1]</span></span></span></a></blockquote><a href="https://d.docs.live.net/6a09c1440da84011/_Education/Fuller%20Theological%20Seminary%20(FTS)/Courses/Old_Testament_Introduction_OT500/Discussions_Course_.docx#_ftn1" name="_ftnref1" title=""><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif; font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 107%; mso-ansi-language: EN-US; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-language: HE; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-fareast-font-family: Aptos; mso-fareast-language: EN-US; mso-fareast-theme-font: minor-latin; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;"></span></span><!--[endif]--></span></a><o:p></o:p><p></p>

<p class="MsoNormal">My name is <a>Geula </a><span class="MsoCommentReference"><span style="font-size: 8.0pt; line-height: 107%;"><!--[if !supportAnnotations]--><a class="msocomanchor" href="https://d.docs.live.net/6a09c1440da84011/_Education/Fuller%20Theological%20Seminary%20(FTS)/Courses/Old_Testament_Introduction_OT500/Discussions_Course_.docx#_msocom_1" id="_anchor_1" language="JavaScript" name="_msoanchor_1">[DGW1]</a><!--[endif]-->&nbsp;</span></span>(<span dir="RTL" lang="HE">גְּאוּלָה</span><span dir="LTR"></span><span dir="LTR"></span><span dir="LTR"></span><span dir="LTR"></span>), and I was only about ten when they took many
of my friends to Babylon. As a girl, I wasn’t allowed to enjoy the learning of
the boys, but I knew Daniel and the three shovavim that would end up following
him. Daniels younger brothers were my friends. They even taught me to read
Hebrew by scratching the letters into the dirt. They agreed it was a silly rule
and we all thought we’d change it when I grew up and made the rules. That’s
when Babylon came, defeated our soldiers, and our king switched allegiances.
Babylon took many of my friends. My brother Aharon was killed that day, with so
many others, before the king switched sides. The boys were taken during the
first deportation, while I remained in Jerusalem under the vassal king,
Jehoiakim. <o:p></o:p></p>

<p class="MsoNormal">Loosing many of my friends, especially the ‘smart’ ones was
hard. I still practiced my letters when nobody was looking, brushing it away
quickly if any adults came looking. After the breaking, one day, I looked down
the road and Aliyah’s parents were walking toward our bakery. I expected to see
Aliyah, but she wouldn’t be there, then I remembered she was taken, and my
chest ached. Her parents’ shoulders were slumped forward, their faces seemed hollow
and sunken. I offered them a bite of our freshest bread. I think they saw her
in me, because Aliyah’s ima would jostle my hair and offer a tight smile, then
wipe her eyes a little before she would turn away to speak with my parents. When
she did that, my eyes burned, my chest felt tight, like I couldn’t breath, but
I shoved it down, to be strong for Aliyah’s ima. Most days, though, I just felt
numb, like my laughter had disappeared. I helped around the bakery, eventually
taking it over as I got older.<o:p></o:p></p>

<p class="MsoNormal">The adults whispered about how the Assyrians took the
Northern Kingdom of Israel away many generations ago, but they never believed
it could happen to us, not in Yehuda, especially not in Yerushalaym, the very
home of HaShem. Then again, my parents argued bitterly with The Cohanim, that
if they had not allowed all of the other Elohim such prominence in our high
places, HaShem would not have allowed Babylon to come in the first place. The Cohanim
waved them off dismissively. <o:p></o:p></p>

<p class="MsoNormal">In many ways, life seemed to carry on normally, until I was
18. I was running the bakery with my ima when some the shofar began blowing all
throughout the city, and men came running down the streets yelling that the Babylonians
had come, with Chaldeans, Aram, Moab, and Ammonites as well. People began
throwing closed their shops, locking their doors, and looking for the hiding
places we’d dug out in case this day ever came. I couldn’t move, I was too numb,
too frozen. I watched the chaos begin around me, while my ima and I just stood
there, covered in flour dust. Eventually, aba came into the room and found us
just standing there, and he shook us into movement. We closed everything, and
went into hiding. We stayed there for months, eating what bread we had stored,
making little cakes over dung fires from our storage reserves. We heard about
horrible things people were doing in other parts of the city just to eat something.
<o:p></o:p></p>

<p class="MsoNormal">The Vassal King, Jehoiakim thought he would ignore the Tributes
we owed to Babylon after he initially switched allegiances from Egypt to
Babylon. Nebuchadnezzar didn’t like that much and showed up outside our walls.
It’s been almost a year now, and we have no food left. We suck on bread sacks,
hoping for a bit of the stale flour. My Parents have discussed sneaking passed
the city guards to surrender to the Babylonians, because that couldn’t be worse
then living in the city, could it? Then, we got word this evening that Jehoiakim
died mysteriously, nobody was very upset about it or asked too many questions. The
guards opened the gates and let the invaders come in, surrendering to them,
placing the body of Jehoiakim in front of the gate. What will this mean for me?
What will happen to us? Could it be worse than starving to death in my own
city, the city of HaShem, Yerushalaym? I will lay myself on HaShem’s mercy,
perhaps I can find favor in my new home, when they take me away, as long as stay
faithful to Adonai, like my friends Daniel is rumored to have done.<o:p></o:p></p>

<p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>

<p class="MsoNormal"></p><blockquote>“<i>And these are the
words of the letter that Jeremiah the prophet sent from Jerusalem to the
remainder of the exiles, and to the priests, and to the prophets, and to all
the people whom Nebuchadnezzar had deported from Jerusalem to Babylon… “Thus
says Yahweh of hosts, the God of Israel, to all the exiles whom I have deported
from Jerusalem to Babylon, ‘Build houses and live in them, and plant gardens
and eat their fruit. Take wives and father sons and daughters, and take for
your sons wives, and give your daughters to men that they may bear sons and
daughters, and multiply there, and you must not be few. And seek the prosperity
of the city where I have deported you, and pray on behalf of it to Yahweh, for
in its prosperity you will have prosperity.’ For thus says Yahweh of hosts, the
God of Israel, ‘Do not let your prophets who are in your midst, and your
diviners, deceive you, and you must not listen to your dreams that you are
causing them to dream. For they are prophesying falsely to you in my name; I
have not sent them,’ declares Yahweh. For thus says Yahweh, ‘As soon as the
time has passed, seventy years for Babylon, I will attend to you, and I will
fulfill my good word to you, to bring you back to this place. For I know the
plans that I am planning concerning you,’ declares Yahweh, ‘plans for
prosperity and not for harm, to give to you a future and a hope. Then when you
call me, and you come and pray to me, then I will listen to you. When you
search for me, then you will find me, if you seek me with all your heart. And I
will let myself be found by you,’ declares Yahweh, ‘and I will restore your
fortunes, and I will gather you from all the nations and from all the places to
which I have driven you,’ declares Yahweh, ‘and I will bring you back to the
place from which I deported you.’</i>” (Jeremiah 29:1–14, LEB)</blockquote><i><o:p></o:p></i><p></p>

<p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>

<div><!--[if !supportFootnotes]--><br clear="all" />

<hr align="left" size="1" width="33%" />

<!--[endif]-->

<div id="ftn1">

<p class="MsoFootnoteText"><a href="https://d.docs.live.net/6a09c1440da84011/_Education/Fuller%20Theological%20Seminary%20(FTS)/Courses/Old_Testament_Introduction_OT500/Discussions_Course_.docx#_ftnref1" name="_ftn1" title=""><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><!--[if !supportFootnotes]--><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="font-family: Palatino; font-size: 10.0pt; line-height: 107%; mso-ansi-language: EN-US; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-language: AR-SA; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-fareast-language: EN-US;">[1]</span></span><!--[endif]--></span></a>
<!--[if supportFields]><span style='mso-element:field-begin'></span><span
style='mso-spacerun:yes'> </span>ADDIN ZOTERO_ITEM CSL_CITATION
{&quot;citationID&quot;:&quot;Uet3TyXz&quot;,&quot;properties&quot;:{&quot;formattedCitation&quot;:&quot;{\\i{}The
Lexham English Bible (LEB), Fourth Edition}, Logo Bible Software, Harris, W.
H., III, Ritzema, E., Brannan, R., Mangum, D., Dunham, J., Reimer, J. A., &amp;
Wierenga, M. (Eds.) (Bellingham, WA: Lexham Press, 2010), Referenced during
this reflection: Daniel 1:1\\uc0\\u8211{}2, 2 Kings 23:34\\uc0\\u8211{}24:7, 2
Chronicles 36:4\\uc0\\u8211{}8, Jeremiah 22:18\\uc0\\u8211{}19,
29:4\\uc0\\u8211{}7, 36:23, http://www.lexhampress.com.&quot;,&quot;plainCitation&quot;:&quot;The
Lexham English Bible (LEB), Fourth Edition, Logo Bible Software, Harris, W. H.,
III, Ritzema, E., Brannan, R., Mangum, D., Dunham, J., Reimer, J. A., &amp;
Wierenga, M. (Eds.) (Bellingham, WA: Lexham Press, 2010), Referenced during
this reflection: Daniel 1:1–2, 2 Kings 23:34–24:7, 2 Chronicles 36:4–8,
Jeremiah 22:18–19, 29:4–7, 36:23,
http://www.lexhampress.com.&quot;,&quot;noteIndex&quot;:2},&quot;citationItems&quot;:[{&quot;id&quot;:49,&quot;uris&quot;:[&quot;http://zotero.org/users/6953150/items/2TDD6GYW&quot;],&quot;itemData&quot;:{&quot;id&quot;:49,&quot;type&quot;:&quot;book&quot;,&quot;collection-title&quot;:&quot;Harris,
W. H., III, Ritzema, E., Brannan, R., Mangum, D., Dunham, J., Reimer, J. A.,
&amp; Wierenga, M. (Eds.)&quot;,&quot;edition&quot;:&quot;Logo Bible Software&quot;,&quot;event-place&quot;:&quot;Bellingham,
WA&quot;,&quot;publisher&quot;:&quot;Lexham
Press&quot;,&quot;publisher-place&quot;:&quot;Bellingham,
WA&quot;,&quot;source&quot;:&quot;Logo Bible
Software&quot;,&quot;title&quot;:&quot;The Lexham English Bible (LEB), Fourth
Edition&quot;,&quot;title-short&quot;:&quot;LEB&quot;,&quot;URL&quot;:&quot;http://www.lexhampress.com&quot;,&quot;issued&quot;:{&quot;date-parts&quot;:[[&quot;2010&quot;]],&quot;season&quot;:&quot;Copyright<span
style='mso-spacerun:yes'>  </span>, 2012, 2013 Lexham
Press&quot;}},&quot;locator&quot;:&quot;Referenced during this reflection:
Daniel 1:1–2, 2 Kings 23:34–24:7, 2 Chronicles 36:4–8, Jeremiah 22:18–19, 29:4–7,
36:23
&quot;,&quot;label&quot;:&quot;page&quot;}],&quot;schema&quot;:&quot;https://github.com/citation-style-language/schema/raw/master/csl-citation.json&quot;}
<span style='mso-element:field-separator'></span><![endif]--><i>The Lexham
English Bible (LEB), Fourth Edition</i>, Logo Bible Software, Harris, W. H.,
III, Ritzema, E., Brannan, R., Mangum, D., Dunham, J., Reimer, J. A., &amp;
Wierenga, M. (Eds.) (Bellingham, WA: Lexham Press, 2010), Referenced during
this reflection: Daniel 1:1–2, 2 Kings 23:34–24:7, 2 Chronicles 36:4–8,
Jeremiah 22:18–19, 29:4–7, 36:23, http://www.lexhampress.com.<!--[if supportFields]><span
style='mso-element:field-end'></span><![endif]--><o:p></o:p></p>

</div>

</div>

<div><!--[if !supportAnnotations]-->

<hr align="left" class="msocomoff" size="1" width="33%" />

<!--[endif]-->

<div><!--[if !supportAnnotations]-->

<div class="msocomtxt" id="_com_1" language="JavaScript"><!--[endif]--><!--[if !supportAnnotations]--><a name="_msocom_1"></a><!--[endif]-->

<p class="MsoCommentText"><span class="MsoCommentReference"><span style="font-size: 8.0pt;">&nbsp;<!--[if !supportAnnotations]--><a class="msocomoff" href="https://d.docs.live.net/6a09c1440da84011/_Education/Fuller%20Theological%20Seminary%20(FTS)/Courses/Old_Testament_Introduction_OT500/Discussions_Course_.docx#_msoanchor_1">[DGW1]</a><!--[endif]--></span></span>A
Hebrew name that represents <b>"return"</b> or <b>"end of an
era"</b> is <b>"Geula" (<span dir="RTL" lang="HE">גְּאוּלָה</span><span dir="LTR"></span><span dir="LTR"></span>)</b>.<o:p></o:p></p>

<p class="MsoCommentText"><b>Geula (<span dir="RTL" lang="HE">גְּאוּלָה</span><span dir="LTR"></span><span dir="LTR"></span>)</b><o:p></o:p></p>

<p class="MsoCommentText" style="margin-left: .25in; mso-list: l2 level1 lfo1; text-indent: -.25in;"><!--[if !supportLists]--><span style="font-family: Symbol; mso-bidi-font-family: Symbol; mso-fareast-font-family: Symbol;">·<span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; font-feature-settings: normal; font-kerning: auto; font-optical-sizing: auto; font-size-adjust: none; font-size: 7pt; font-stretch: normal; font-variant-alternates: normal; font-variant-east-asian: normal; font-variant-numeric: normal; font-variant-position: normal; font-variation-settings: normal; line-height: normal;">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
</span></span><!--[endif]--><span dir="LTR"></span><b>Meaning:</b> The name <i>Geula</i>
means <b>"redemption"</b> or <b>"deliverance"</b> in
Hebrew.<o:p></o:p></p>

<p class="MsoCommentText" style="margin-left: .25in; mso-list: l2 level1 lfo1; text-indent: -.25in;"><!--[if !supportLists]--><span style="font-family: Symbol; mso-bidi-font-family: Symbol; mso-fareast-font-family: Symbol;">·<span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; font-feature-settings: normal; font-kerning: auto; font-optical-sizing: auto; font-size-adjust: none; font-size: 7pt; font-stretch: normal; font-variant-alternates: normal; font-variant-east-asian: normal; font-variant-numeric: normal; font-variant-position: normal; font-variation-settings: normal; line-height: normal;">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
</span></span><!--[endif]--><span dir="LTR"></span><b>Significance:</b> In
Jewish tradition, <i>Geula</i> signifies the act of being redeemed or liberated
from a state of exile or hardship. It often refers to the prophesied future
redemption of the Jewish people and the end of a difficult period, symbolizing
both a <b>return</b> to a promised state and the <b>end of an era</b> of
suffering.<o:p></o:p></p>

<p class="MsoCommentText" style="margin-left: .25in; mso-list: l2 level1 lfo1; text-indent: -.25in;"><!--[if !supportLists]--><span style="font-family: Symbol; mso-bidi-font-family: Symbol; mso-fareast-font-family: Symbol;">·<span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; font-feature-settings: normal; font-kerning: auto; font-optical-sizing: auto; font-size-adjust: none; font-size: 7pt; font-stretch: normal; font-variant-alternates: normal; font-variant-east-asian: normal; font-variant-numeric: normal; font-variant-position: normal; font-variation-settings: normal; line-height: normal;">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
</span></span><!--[endif]--><span dir="LTR"></span><b>Usage:</b> <i>Geula</i>
is used as a feminine given name in Hebrew-speaking communities.<o:p></o:p></p>

<p class="MsoCommentText"><b>Contextual Significance</b><o:p></o:p></p>

<p class="MsoCommentText" style="margin-left: .25in; mso-list: l1 level1 lfo2; text-indent: -.25in;"><!--[if !supportLists]--><span style="font-family: Symbol; mso-bidi-font-family: Symbol; mso-fareast-font-family: Symbol;">·<span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; font-feature-settings: normal; font-kerning: auto; font-optical-sizing: auto; font-size-adjust: none; font-size: 7pt; font-stretch: normal; font-variant-alternates: normal; font-variant-east-asian: normal; font-variant-numeric: normal; font-variant-position: normal; font-variation-settings: normal; line-height: normal;">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
</span></span><!--[endif]--><span dir="LTR"></span><b>Biblical References:</b>
The concept of <i>Geula</i> is rooted in the Hebrew Bible, where it denotes
God's saving actions on behalf of His people.<o:p></o:p></p>

<p class="MsoCommentText" style="margin-left: .25in; mso-list: l1 level1 lfo2; text-indent: -.25in;"><!--[if !supportLists]--><span style="font-family: Symbol; mso-bidi-font-family: Symbol; mso-fareast-font-family: Symbol;">·<span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; font-feature-settings: normal; font-kerning: auto; font-optical-sizing: auto; font-size-adjust: none; font-size: 7pt; font-stretch: normal; font-variant-alternates: normal; font-variant-east-asian: normal; font-variant-numeric: normal; font-variant-position: normal; font-variation-settings: normal; line-height: normal;">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
</span></span><!--[endif]--><span dir="LTR"></span><b>Cultural Meaning:</b>
The name embodies hope, renewal, and the transition into a new and better
phase, making it synonymous with the <b>end of an era</b> and the beginning of
another.<o:p></o:p></p>

<p class="MsoCommentText"><b>Alternative Names</b><o:p></o:p></p>

<p class="MsoCommentText" style="margin-left: .25in; mso-list: l0 level1 lfo3; text-indent: -.25in;"><!--[if !supportLists]--><span style="font-family: Symbol; mso-bidi-font-family: Symbol; mso-fareast-font-family: Symbol;">·<span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; font-feature-settings: normal; font-kerning: auto; font-optical-sizing: auto; font-size-adjust: none; font-size: 7pt; font-stretch: normal; font-variant-alternates: normal; font-variant-east-asian: normal; font-variant-numeric: normal; font-variant-position: normal; font-variation-settings: normal; line-height: normal;">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
</span></span><!--[endif]--><span dir="LTR"></span><b>Shuv (<span dir="RTL" lang="HE">שׁוּב</span><span dir="LTR"></span><span dir="LTR"></span>):</b> Derived
from the Hebrew root meaning <b>"return,"</b> though it's less
commonly used as a given name.<o:p></o:p></p>

<p class="MsoCommentText" style="margin-left: .25in; mso-list: l0 level1 lfo3; text-indent: -.25in;"><!--[if !supportLists]--><span style="font-family: Symbol; mso-bidi-font-family: Symbol; mso-fareast-font-family: Symbol;">·<span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; font-feature-settings: normal; font-kerning: auto; font-optical-sizing: auto; font-size-adjust: none; font-size: 7pt; font-stretch: normal; font-variant-alternates: normal; font-variant-east-asian: normal; font-variant-numeric: normal; font-variant-position: normal; font-variation-settings: normal; line-height: normal;">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
</span></span><!--[endif]--><span dir="LTR"></span><b>Nehemiah (<span dir="RTL" lang="HE">נְחֶמְיָה</span><span dir="LTR"></span><span dir="LTR"></span>):</b>
Meaning <b>"God comforts,"</b> associated with the return to
Jerusalem and rebuilding after the Babylonian exile.<o:p></o:p></p>

<p class="MsoCommentText" style="margin-left: .25in; mso-list: l0 level1 lfo3; text-indent: -.25in;"><!--[if !supportLists]--><span style="font-family: Symbol; mso-bidi-font-family: Symbol; mso-fareast-font-family: Symbol;">·<span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; font-feature-settings: normal; font-kerning: auto; font-optical-sizing: auto; font-size-adjust: none; font-size: 7pt; font-stretch: normal; font-variant-alternates: normal; font-variant-east-asian: normal; font-variant-numeric: normal; font-variant-position: normal; font-variation-settings: normal; line-height: normal;">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
</span></span><!--[endif]--><span dir="LTR"></span><b>Aliyah (<span dir="RTL" lang="HE">עֲלִיָּה</span><span dir="LTR"></span><span dir="LTR"></span>):</b> Meaning
<b>"ascent"</b> or <b>"going up,"</b> commonly used to
refer to the act of immigrating to Israel, symbolizing a <b>return</b> to the
homeland.<o:p></o:p></p>

<!--[if !supportAnnotations]--></div>

<!--[endif]--></div>

</div>
<br />
<br />
<br />
<br />
<br />
<div id="mc_embed_signup">
<form action="https://darrellwolfe.us3.list-manage.com/subscribe/post?u=979cb0db6ca74343e82a059ba&amp;id=14df3ba97b" class="validate" id="mc-embedded-subscribe-form" method="post" name="mc-embedded-subscribe-form" novalidate="" target="_blank">
<div id="mc_embed_signup_scroll">
<label>Subscribe Now - Pursue a No Hiding Lifestyle</label>&nbsp;<input class="email" id="mce-EMAIL" name="EMAIL" required="" type="email" value="" /><br />
<div aria-hidden="true" style="left: -5000px; position: absolute;">
<input name="b_979cb0db6ca74343e82a059ba_14df3ba97b" tabindex="-1" type="text" value="" /></div>
<div class="clear">
<input class="button" id="mc-embedded-subscribe" name="subscribe" type="submit" value="Subscribe" /></div>
</div>
</form>
</div>
<br />
<span style="font-family: &quot;tempus sans itc&quot;;">Shalom: Live Long and Prosper!</span><br />
<div class="MsoNormal">
<span style="font-family: &quot;tempus sans itc&quot;; font-size: 18pt;">Darrell Wolfe (DG Wolfe)</span></div>
<div class="MsoNormal">
<span style="font-family: &quot;tempus sans itc&quot;;">Storyteller | Writer | Thinker | Consultant @&nbsp;<a href="http://www.darrellwolfe.com/p/start-here-about_12.html" target="_blank">DarrellWolfe.com</a></span><br />
<br />
<span style="font-family: &quot;tempus sans itc&quot;;"><a href="https://www.gallupstrengthscenter.com/" target="_blank">Clifton StrengthsFinder</a>: Intellection, Learner,&nbsp;</span><span style="font-family: &quot;tempus sans itc&quot;;">Ideation,</span><span style="font-family: &quot;tempus sans itc&quot;;">&nbsp;Achiever, Input</span><br />
<span style="font-family: &quot;tempus sans itc&quot;;"><a href="https://www.16personalities.com/infj-personality" target="_blank">16Personalities</a> (Myers-Briggs Type): INFJ</span><br />
<span style="font-family: &quot;tempus sans itc&quot;;"><br /></span>
<span style="font-family: &quot;tempus sans itc&quot;;"><br /></span></div>
